summary: Yann LeCun argues that current Auto-Regressive LLMs will never achieve human-level intelligence because they lack persistent memory, reasoning, and physical intuition. He proposes Objective-Driven AI and Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) that learn world models by predicting in abstract representation spaces rather than pixel spaces. He also strongly advocates for open-source AI as essential for cultural diversity and democracy.

principles.statement: We will never reach human-level intelligence by just training on text.
principles.rationale: A 4-year-old child processes roughly the same amount of data (in bytes) through their optic nerves as the biggest LLMs process from the entire public internet. Text lacks the bandwidth and physical grounding required to learn common sense.
principles.source_quote: we're never going to reach anything close to human level intelligence by just training on text it's just not going to happen

principles.statement: The essence of intelligence and science is finding good representations to make predictions.
principles.rationale: Predicting complex phenomena (like a planet's trajectory) doesn't require knowing every micro-detail; it only requires a few abstract numbers. AI must similarly learn abstract representations of the world to predict future states.
principles.source_quote: finding a good representation of something or phenomenon so that you can make prediction it's really the essence of science really

principles.statement: AI platforms must be open source to preserve cultural diversity and democracy.
principles.rationale: Future AI assistants will mediate all our digital interactions and constitute a repository of human knowledge. This infrastructure cannot be controlled by a single company on the US coasts; it requires global contributions to understand all languages, cultures, and value systems.
principles.source_quote: open source AI is not just a good idea it's necessary for cultural diversity perhaps even the preservation of democracy

principles.statement: Superintelligent machines will be under our control because they will be objective-driven.
principles.rationale: We will design AI systems with specific goals and guardrails. Just as human leaders successfully manage employees who are smarter than them without being dominated, humanity can safely manage smarter machines.
principles.source_quote: machines will surpass human intelligence but they will be under control because they will be objective driven

frameworks.name: Objective-Driven AI Architecture (Inference by Optimization)
frameworks.when_to_apply: When building AI systems that need to reason, plan, and safely accomplish new tasks without requiring task-specific learning.
steps: Make an observation of the world and use a perception system to extract a representation of the current state.
Combine the perceived state with information from persistent memory.
Feed a hypothesized sequence of actions into a learned 'World Model' to predict the future state or trajectory of the world.
Evaluate the predicted trajectory using objective functions (one measuring goal completion, others acting as safety guardrails).
Backpropagate gradients through the differentiable modules to find and execute the action sequence that minimizes the cost/objectives.
frameworks.source_quote: the inference process... consist in finding the action sequence that minimizes those objectives... you back propagate gradient through them and you update the action sequence to GR in descent

mental_models.name: Moravec's Paradox
mental_models.description: The observation that tasks appearing trivial to humans (like clearing a table) are incredibly difficult for machines, while high-level abstract thinking (like manipulating language or playing chess) is relatively easy for machines.
mental_models.example: A 10-year-old can learn to clear a dinner table and fill a dishwasher in one shot without practice, while AI systems and household robots still completely fail at this.

mental_models.name: Hierarchical Planning
mental_models.description: Breaking down complex goals into multiple levels of abstraction rather than planning every micro-action from the start. AI currently lacks this capability.
mental_models.example: When planning a trip from New York to Paris, you plan at a high level ('go to the airport', 'catch a plane') rather than calculating the millisecond-by-millisecond low-level muscle control required to stand up from your chair.

mental_models.name: The Smart Employee Analogy
mental_models.description: A lens for reasoning about AI safety: creating an entity smarter than yourself does not inherently mean it will try to dominate or kill you.
mental_models.example: Leaders in industry and academia regularly work with and manage people who are smarter than them, without those smarter employees attempting to take over.

heuristics: Abandon generative models in favor of joint embedding predictive architectures (JEPAs).
Predict in representation space, not in pixel space.
When something surprising happens, that means your world model is wrong.

signature_quotes.text: we're never going to reach anything close to human level intelligence by just training on text it's just not going to happen
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the data bandwidth limits of LLMs compared to the visual data a human child processes.

signature_quotes.text: finding a good representation of something or phenomenon so that you can make prediction it's really the essence of science really
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why AI should predict in abstract representation spaces rather than exact pixels.

signature_quotes.text: open source AI is not just a good idea it's necessary for cultural diversity perhaps even the preservation of democracy
signature_quotes.context: Arguing against closed, proprietary AI platforms controlled by a few coastal US companies.

signature_quotes.text: machines will surpass human intelligence but they will be under control because they will be objective driven
signature_quotes.context: Addressing existential risk and AI safety concerns at the end of his talk.

signature_quotes.text: I'm telling you a number of different things here abandon generative models... abandon probic models... abandon contrastive methods... and also reinforcement learning... so I'm not very popular at the moment
signature_quotes.context: Acknowledging that his proposed architecture goes against the four most popular pillars of current machine learning.

anti_patterns.description: Trying to learn world models by training generative models to predict the exact pixels of future video frames.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: The physical world is highly unpredictable at the micro-level. A system cannot accurately predict the exact texture, lighting, or pixel-level details of a room's future state, making the task impossible and failing to capture true physical intuition.

anti_patterns.description: Believing Auto-Regressive LLMs will lead to human-level AI.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: LLMs lack persistent memory, cannot plan complex action sequences, do not perform real reasoning, and only work on discrete tokens (ignoring the vast, continuous physical reality).

anti_patterns.description: Fearing that AI will suddenly become superintelligent overnight and wipe out humanity.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Progress in AI is a progressive evolution, not a sudden switch. Furthermore, future systems will be objective-driven, meaning they will be controlled by the goals and guardrails we set for them.
